stem: A biased coin lands heads with probability \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \) on each flip, independently. The coin is flipped [[n]] times. What is the probability of getting exactly [[k]] heads?
solution: The number of heads is binomial, so \( P(X = [[k]]) = \binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]}\left(\frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[k]]}\left(1 - \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[n]] - [[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the binomial coefficient \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]} \) that counts which flips are heads.
This omits the failure factor \( (1-p)^{[[n]] - [[k]]} \) for the tails.
This raises the success probability to the power [[n]] instead of [[k]].
This is the probability of all [[n]] flips being heads.
This uses first powers instead of the exponents [[k]] and \( [[n]] - [[k]] \).
This is the complementary probability of NOT getting exactly [[k]] heads.

stem: A certain free-throw shooter makes each attempt independently with probability \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \). Out of [[n]] attempts, what is the probability that the shooter makes AT LEAST [[k]] of them?

answer_spec.expr: binomial([[n]], i) * Rational([[pn]], [[pd]]) ** i * (1 - Rational([[pn]], [[pd]])) ** ([[n]] - i)

solution: Sum the exact-count probabilities from [[k]] through [[n]] makes: \( P(X \ge [[k]]) = \sum_{i=[[k]]}^{[[n]]} \binom{[[n]]}{i}\left(\frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{i}\left(1 - \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[n]]-i} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives only the probability of EXACTLY [[k]] makes, omitting all the higher counts.
This treats the event as needing every one of the first [[k]] attempts to succeed, ignoring the binomial structure.
This complements a single-attempt-style probability rather than summing the tail of the binomial distribution.
This is just the naive fraction of attempts needed, not a probability computed from the distribution.
This attempts a complement using the wrong boundary (\( [[k]]-1 \) makes) with a sign/direction error.
This is the probability of getting exactly [[k]] successes ANd exactly [[n]]-[[k]] more, i.e. all [[n]] attempts landing on probability [[pn]]/[[pd]] raised to [[n]], not a valid decomposition.

stem: A machine part fails a quality test independently with probability \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \). Out of [[n]] parts tested, what is the probability that NO MORE THAN [[k]] of them fail?

answer_spec.expr: binomial([[n]], i) * Rational([[pn]], [[pd]]) ** i * (1 - Rational([[pn]], [[pd]])) ** ([[n]] - i)

solution: Sum the exact-count probabilities from 0 through [[k]] failures: \( P(X \le [[k]]) = \sum_{i=0}^{[[k]]} \binom{[[n]]}{i}\left(\frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{i}\left(1 - \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[n]]-i} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives only the probability of EXACTLY [[k]] failures, omitting the counts below [[k]].
This complements a single-attempt-style probability rather than the correct cumulative sum.
This is the probability of zero failures out of [[n]], ignoring that up to [[k]] failures are also allowed.
This is just the naive fraction of allowed failures, not a probability computed from the distribution.
This is the complementary tail \( P(X > [[k]]) \) using the exact-[[k]] term instead of the true cumulative sum.
This swaps the roles of the success and failure probabilities in the exact-count formula.

stem: A machine independently produces a defective unit with probability \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \) on each of [[n]] production runs. What is the probability that the total number of defective units is EVEN (counting zero as even)?
solution: Using the identity \( P(\text{even count}) = \frac{1 + (1-2p)^{[[n]]}}{2} \) (from expanding \( (q+p)^{[[n]]} \) and \( (q-p)^{[[n]]} \) and averaging), with \( p = \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \), the probability is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the probability of an ODD count, the complementary parity.
This is the probability that NO units are defective, a single term rather than the full even-parity sum.
This is the naive guess that even and odd counts are equally likely, which is only true when \( p = 1/2 \).
This forgets to divide by 2 when averaging the two binomial expansions.
This computes the odd-count probability via the complement, but with a sign error in applying it.
This flips the sign inside the power, using \( (2p-1)^{[[n]]} \) instead of \( (1-2p)^{[[n]]} \) (these agree only when [[n]] is even).

stem: On each independent trial, the probability of success is \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \). Trials continue until the [[r]]th success occurs. What is the probability that the [[r]]th success happens on exactly trial number [[n]]?
solution: For the [[r]]th success to fall exactly on trial [[n]], exactly \( [[r]] - 1 \) of the first \( [[n]] - 1 \) trials must succeed (any arrangement) and trial [[n]] itself must succeed: \( \binom{[[n]]-1}{[[r]]-1}\left(\frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[r]]}\left(1-\frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[n]]-[[r]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[r]]} \), the ordinary binomial coefficient over all [[n]] trials, instead of restricting to the first \( [[n]]-1 \) trials before the final guaranteed success.
This drops the failure factor for the trials among the first \( [[n]]-1 \) that were NOT successes.
This counts arrangements of [[r]] successes among the first \( [[n]]-1 \) trials instead of \( [[r]]-1 \), miscounting since the last trial is fixed as a success.
This is the ordinary binomial probability of exactly [[r]] successes in [[n]] trials in any order, not requiring the last trial to be the [[r]]th success.
This uses one fewer failure than needed in the exponent, an off-by-one error in the number of failed trials.
This is a naive ratio of successes needed to trials available, not a probability computed from the distribution.
